title_of_original: SA.39182 (2017/C) (ex 2017/NN) (ex 2014/CP) Alleged illegal aid to Tartu Agro AS implemented by Estonia
case_description: [1] In 2013, the Ministry of Agriculture of Lithuania, acting on behalf of the State, [2] launched a restricted tender procedure to lease 2,800 hectares of state-owned agricultural land located in the central Panevėžys region. [3] The land, owned by the Lithuanian State and managed by the Ministry’s Land Administration Division, [4] was suitable for crop farming and dairy operations but did not include any built facilities. [5] The tender notice was published in the official journal, [6] but it included a restrictive clause: bidders were required to maintain the same type of agricultural activities that had previously been carried out on the land, [7] specifically dairy farming and cereal cultivation. [8] These activities had been conducted by AgroBaltic UAB, a private agricultural company, under a temporary land use agreement that had expired shortly before the tender was launched. [9] Two bids were received. [10] AgroBaltic UAB offered a fixed annual rent of €6 per hectare, [11] along with a commitment to carry out basic drainage and soil maintenance. [12] The second bidder was excluded for failing to provide a notarized certificate of technical qualifications. [13] The Ministry did not commission any independent market valuation of the land, [14] nor did it consult recent data on comparable farmland leases in the region. [15] A 20-year lease was awarded to AgroBaltic UAB on these terms. [16] The contract included no automatic rent adjustment clause, [17] and while it permitted price revision, this mechanism was never used. [18] Over the lease period, public data from the Lithuanian Land Fund and regional land auctions show that market rents for comparable farmland typically ranged between €40–90 per hectare per year. [19] In addition, the lease allowed AgroBaltic UAB to deduct the cost of land maintenance activities (e.g., weed control and minor ditch repair) from its rental payments. [20] These terms were not disclosed in the published tender documents and were not offered to other operators. [21] The land remains fully owned by the Lithuanian State, [22] and the lease was signed by senior Ministry officials in accordance with domestic administrative procedures. [23] The lease arrangement was not notified to the European Commission.
